Q1: I usually organized my study routine around listening to lectures and podcasts, since I learn best through audio. I'd set aside chunks of time throughout the week, maybe 4-5 hours a day, where I'd have course content playing while I took notes. Honestly, I tried to front-load my studying earlier in the week so I wouldn't be scrambling, but it didn't always work out that way. I'd often batch similar tasks together, like watching all the video lectures for one topic before moving on.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep some consistency, like studying in the mornings when I'm more alert, but deadlines would throw everything off. When assignments were coming up, I'd suddenly be putting in way more hours than usual. Some weeks I barely touched the course materials, other weeks I was completely immersed.
Q3: Usually an upcoming deadline or the need to check if new content had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see my grades or check announcements. To be honest, I wasn't one of those students who logged in daily just to browse around—I needed a specific reason to be there. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more often because I wanted to see my progress.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day—it was always something I went to with a purpose and then left. I guess around midterms it became more habitual because I was constantly checking for resources and feedback. But that routine faded once the pressure lifted. It felt more like a tool I used when needed rather than a space I inhabited.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the concepts. The recorded lectures with good audio quality were gold—I'd sometimes play them at 1.5x speed while reviewing my notes. Any resources that let me hear explanations rather than just read them were the most valuable to me.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums. I only posted twice the whole semester because typing out my thoughts felt tedious and I didn't feel like I was getting much from reading other people's posts. The reading materials I'd often skim or download and never look at again. If something wasn't audio-based or directly tied to an assignment, it usually fell to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. The gamified tasks were actually pretty engaging because they gave me immediate feedback and made progress feel tangible. I also valued anything that respected my time—if something was efficient and well-designed, I was more likely to engage with it fully.
Q8: Optional activities felt like nice-to-haves that I rarely actually got to. Required activities had this urgency attached to them that made me prioritize them automatically. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll get to that later' and then never did because something required would come up. The gamified optional tasks were different though—they felt more like a game than homework, so I actually did some of those.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos that covered similar topics when I needed concepts explained differently. My personal notes became my main study resource after I'd processed the lectures, so a lot of my studying happened completely offline with just my notebook and highlighters.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but it wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. Like when I'd re-listen to downloaded lectures three or four times while cooking dinner, or when I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes before an exam. I also had study sessions with a few classmates where we'd discuss concepts verbally—that was huge for my learning but totally invisible to the LMS.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download lecture videos or audio files once and then access them repeatedly offline. My phone was basically my portable classroom. I'd watch or listen to something once on the LMS to download it, then that single access would turn into hours of offline studying. The LMS would only show that one initial access even though I engaged with the material many times.
Q12: Around week six or seven, I hit a wall. I was taking twenty online courses and the workload just became overwhelming. I felt bored with the routine and my motivation dipped significantly. There was also a period when I was dealing with some personal stress that made it hard to focus. My attendance dropped during those times and I was just going through the motions rather than really engaging.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. The competing demands of twenty courses meant I had to constantly triage what got my attention. Sometimes I'd skip the LMS entirely and just study from my notes because opening the platform felt like one more thing to deal with. Confusion about course structure also made me avoid certain sections.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive without actually absorbing information. Sometimes I'd log in, click around aimlessly, and log out without accomplishing anything meaningful. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement in the data but they were basically empty time.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on discussion posts because I found them boring and they felt like busywork. I'd also avoid readings that seemed dense or poorly organized, even when I knew they contained important information. Sometimes I'd postpone starting assignments because the thought of beginning felt overwhelming, even though I knew waiting would make things worse. It's like my brain needed the pressure of a looming deadline to actually get moving.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated everything. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd cram in video watching, resource downloading, and assignment work all at once. Without a deadline, I had very little motivation to engage with the platform. The gamified tasks helped somewhat because they created mini-deadlines and goals, but major assignment due dates were still the primary driver of my activity.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, especially when it was timely and specific. Good feedback on an assignment would motivate me to put more effort into the next one. When feedback was delayed or generic, I felt disconnected from the course and less invested. The immediate feedback from gamified tasks was actually really motivating—it made me want to keep going and improve my scores right away.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact on my participation. When modules were clearly organized and I knew exactly what was expected, I engaged more consistently. But when things felt scattered or the path forward was unclear, I'd disengage and just focus on what was obviously required. The flexibility of online courses was both a blessing and a curse—it let me study on my own schedule but also made it easy to fall behind.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they added some fun to an otherwise monotonous routine. Clear deadlines and well-organized modules helped too. What made it harder was when there were too many resources without clear guidance on priorities, or when the audio quality of lectures was poor. Also, having twenty courses meant my attention was constantly divided, which made deep engagement with any single course difficult.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are searching for something specific or trying to understand confusing material. They might click through lots of resources looking for the right explanation without finding it. Or maybe they're dealing with anxiety and the clicking feels productive even when it isn't. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of struggling rather than thriving—you're working hard but not getting anywhere.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. I performed well on exams because I studied effectively offline, but my LMS activity was relatively low. If you learn well through methods that don't involve the platform—like listening to downloaded content or reviewing personal notes—you can succeed without showing much online engagement. The LMS is just one tool, not the whole picture of how learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when students accessed materials and how often they logged in. They can track completion of specific tasks and time spent on the platform. But they miss all the offline learning—the re-reading, the discussions with classmates, the thinking that happens away from the screen. They also can't measure the quality of engagement, just the quantity. Someone clicking around aimlessly looks the same as someone deeply focused.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's when my brain is actively working on the content. For me, engagement feels like curiosity and challenge mixed together. When I'm truly engaged, time passes quickly and I come away feeling like I actually learned something meaningful.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening to downloaded content repeatedly, or the conversations I had with peers outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my emotional journey—the boredom I felt at times, the anxiety before exams, or the satisfaction when concepts finally clicked. It also wouldn't show that despite my low resilience and moderate stress, I pushed through and earned a distinction. The human struggle behind the grades is invisible in the data.
